name: master indian saag curry
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS. Same core (pestled spinach + indian spices + lime + cheese), but the vegetable/protein slot forks and the fat + pepper decisions follow.

=== SHARED PREP (BOTH VARIANTS) ===

PESTLE THE SPINACH: pound fresh spinach in a mortar and pestle to a rough paste — visible spinach pieces, not smooth. If no mortar, finely dice the spinach with a knife. Do NOT blend or food-process into a smooth puree (see notes on babyfood spinach).

SHARED SPICE STACK: cumin, coriander, ajwain, turmeric, garam masala, cardamom, chili powder, kashmiri chili powder, red chili flakes, amchur powder. Toast in the pan's fat until fragrant.

SHARED FINISH: generous squeeze of fresh lime — non-negotiable in both variants.

=== VARIANT A — POTATO + CAULIFLOWER (VEGETABLE-FORWARD, NO PANEER) ===

A vegetable-forward saag with parboiled potatoes and crispy-baked cauliflower as the structural bulk. Ghee is essential; soft cheese (feta or monterrey jack) rounds it. No fresh peppers here (would pull the dish mediterranean).

INGREDIENTS:
- fresh spinach
- potatoes
- cauliflower
- ghee (essential — see notes)
- vegetable oil (to cut the ghee)
- garlic
- shared spice stack
- feta cheese OR monterrey jack cheese
- lime
- salt

PROCEDURE:
1. PARBOIL THE POTATOES: cube potatoes and parboil in salted water until just-tender-but-still-firm. Drain.
2. PAR-BAKE THE CAULIFLOWER: toss florets in vegetable oil, salt, a pinch of turmeric. Roast at 425°F until deeply browned and crispy at the edges.
3. Pestle the spinach.
4. BUILD THE MASALA: warm ghee cut with vegetable oil in a pan. Add garlic, cook briefly (don't brown). Add the shared spice stack; toast until fragrant.
5. FOLD IN: pestled spinach, parboiled potatoes, crispy cauliflower. Warm through gently.
6. Add crumbled feta OR shredded monterrey jack. Let it soften into the sauce.
7. Finish with lime. Salt to taste.

=== VARIANT B — PANEER (PALAK PANEER, WITH FRESH PEPPERS) ===

Traditional palak paneer, plus fresh peppers. Fried paneer as the protein. Olive oil (not ghee), and no soft/melted cheese — paneer alone carries the dairy role.

INGREDIENTS:
- fresh spinach
- paneer
- olive oil (see notes on skip-the-ghee)
- garlic
- fresno peppers AND/OR bell peppers
- shared spice stack
- lime
- salt

PROCEDURE:
1. Pestle the spinach.
2. CUBE AND FRY THE PANEER — DO THIS FIRST, ALWAYS: cube paneer and shallow-fry in olive oil until golden on all sides. Set the fried paneer aside. See notes on why paneer must be fried BEFORE the masala goes into the pan — this ordering is non-negotiable in variant B.
3. BUILD THE MASALA: in the same pan (with residual paneer-frying oil), add garlic and cook briefly (don't brown). Add finely diced fresno peppers and/or bell peppers, sauté briefly. Add the shared spice stack; toast until fragrant.
4. FOLD IN: pestled spinach and the fried paneer. Warm through gently.
5. Finish with lime. Salt to taste.
notes:
TWO VARIANTS — CHOOSE ONE, DON'T MIX: the variants are structurally opposed on several axes. Don't stack potatoes + paneer + peppers + ghee + olive oil — you'll end up with a muddled dish. Pick one variant and lean into it.

MORTAR-AND-PESTLE APPROACH TO SAAG: this recipe is the mortar-pestle version of saag, as opposed to the blender-and-coconut version (saag-coconut-sauce). I prefer this approach because:
- More spinach per bite. The pestle leaves recognizable spinach pieces in the dish rather than dissolving everything into a smooth sauce.
- Better with structural vegetables (variant A) or fried paneer (variant B) — both want the spinach to coat rather than drown.

SPINACH IS BEST; MIXED GREENS ARE SECONDARY: fresh spinach is the canonical green here. Mixed greens (baby arugula blend, etc.) work in a pinch but read lighter and less structural — spinach's soft leaves pound cleanly into a paste and coat the vegetables/paneer with the right density. Reach for mixed greens only if you have no spinach.

NO BABYFOOD/BLENDED SPINACH — FINELY DICE OR MORTAR-PESTLE ONLY: blender-smooth spinach reads as babyfood — a uniform green paste that erases spinach's leafy character and leaves an unpleasant baby-food-jar mouthfeel. Two acceptable prep paths: (1) mortar-pestle to a ROUGH paste (visible spinach pieces retained), or (2) finely dice with a knife (small pieces distributed through the sauce). Don't blend, don't food-process. If the current mortar-pestle spinach reads too chunky in the finished dish, dice it finer next time — not smoother.

=== VARIANT A NOTES (POTATO + CAULIFLOWER) ===

GHEE IS ESSENTIAL — CUT WITH VEGETABLE OIL: variant A's flavor structure rests on ghee. The nutty depth ghee brings ties the parboiled potatoes, crispy cauliflower, and pestled spinach into a cohesive dish. Straight ghee would smoke off before the masala fries; cut it 50/50 (or 60/40 ghee-heavy) with vegetable oil to bring the smoke point up without losing the ghee character. This is variant-A specific — variant B uses olive oil.

POTATOES PARBOILED, CAULIFLOWER CRISPY-BAKED: same rules as before. Parboiled potatoes hold their shape when they hit the masala and don't turn to mush. Crispy-baked cauliflower (florets tossed in oil + salt + a pinch of turmeric, roasted at 425°F until browned) holds its texture against the wet sauce. Steamed or raw-added cauliflower goes limp; sautéed-from-raw potatoes don't cook through in time.

FETA OR MONTERREY JACK — SOFT CHEESE IS THE MOVE: variant A wants a soft cheese that softens/melts into the sauce, not the fried-paneer structure of variant B. Feta (crumbled) is my current favorite — the brined tang pairs well with the ghee and the parboiled potato. Monterrey jack (shredded) works too and reads more gratin-adjacent (matches the tortilla/quesadilla application well). Pick one; don't stack both.

NO FRESNO / BELL PEPPERS IN VARIANT A: fresh peppers here push the dish toward a mediterranean vegetable-and-cheese register — feta + peppers + potato reads greek-adjacent, and the mediterranean pull fights variant A's saag-with-vegetables identity. Skip the peppers entirely; the ajwain + chili powder + kashmiri chili powder stack handles the aromatic-and-heat slot.

AJWAIN AND THYME NEED RESTRAINT IN VARIANT A — RISK OF READING TOO MEDITERRANEAN: unlike in variant B (paneer, where they're at home), ajwain and thyme in variant A (potato + cauliflower) can push the dish over into mediterranean-vegetable-side territory. The feta + potato + cauliflower base is already flirting with a greek/turkish register; heavy ajwain and thyme on top of that finish the drift. Use ajwain lightly, and skip the thyme in variant A (or use it very sparingly). If you want more herb character, lean on the kashmiri chili powder + cardamom (see cardamom note below) to keep the dish anchored indian.

=== VARIANT B NOTES (PANEER) ===

OLIVE OIL, NOT GHEE: variant B skips the ghee. The paneer already provides the dairy fat character; adding ghee on top makes the dish heavy and overly-rich. Olive oil is the clean fat that lets the paneer and peppers lead. Vegetable oil also fine as a substitute — the point is neutral, not ghee-forward.

FRY THE PANEER FIRST — BEFORE THE MASALA GOES IN: this is the technique note for variant B. Paneer doesn't release much liquid when it hits the pan (unlike, say, chicken or vegetables). If you build the masala first — bloom garlic and peppers in oil, add ground spices — and THEN add raw paneer, the garlic and spices will scorch on the pan bottom before the paneer has any moisture to protect them. The paneer just sits there dry while the aromatics burn underneath. Order matters:
1. Fry the paneer cubes first in olive oil until golden. Set aside.
2. THEN build the masala in the same pan with residual oil (which is now paneer-flavored — a bonus).
3. Add the fried paneer back at the end alongside the spinach.
This is unique to paneer as a protein; a chicken or tofu variant would have enough surface moisture to protect the masala from burning. Not paneer.

AJWAIN AND THYME ARE ESPECIALLY GOOD IN VARIANT B: ajwain (already primary — see shared notes) and thyme (added as secondary here) are especially at home in the paneer variant. Both have a thyme-and-oregano-adjacent character that pairs cleanly with fried paneer's dairy-fried surface. Paneer + ajwain + thyme is one of the more distinctive combinations in this dish family. Toss the thyme into the ground spice mix alongside the ajwain.

FRESNO AND BELL PEPPERS WELCOME: variant B wants fresh peppers. Fresno gives a touch of heat alongside vegetal sweetness; bell peppers are milder and read purely sweet. Finely dice and sauté with the garlic before adding the ground spices. Both together work; either alone works.

NO SOFT CHEESE / NO FETA: the fried paneer is the cheese element in variant B. Adding feta or monterrey jack on top makes it two-cheese-dish territory and muddles the paneer's structural role. Paneer alone here.

NO POTATOES OR CAULIFLOWER IN VARIANT B (BY DEFAULT): variant B is closer to traditional palak paneer — spinach and paneer as the two components. You can add potato or cauliflower if you want, but it starts drifting toward variant A. If you find yourself wanting both, do variant A and skip the paneer.

=== SHARED / CROSS-VARIANT NOTES ===

LIME IS REQUIRED — NON-NEGOTIABLE IN BOTH VARIANTS: alongside cheese and the pestled spinach, the lime finish is what makes this recipe cohere. The brightness cuts the dairy fat and the earthy indian spices. Specifically LIME (not lemon) — lime's more citrus-forward, slightly floral character reads right against the saag; lemon's rounder acid pushes the dish toward a lemon-butter register that doesn't work here. Squeeze fresh lime generously at the finish.

AJWAIN IS PART OF THE SPICE STACK — BUT VARIANT-CONDITIONAL: ajwain (carom seeds) has a thyme-and-oregano-adjacent character with a slight bitter bite. In variant B (paneer) it's a real feature — pairs cleanly with fried paneer and belongs generously. In variant A (potato + cauliflower) it needs restraint — heavy ajwain against the feta + potato + cauliflower base pushes the dish mediterranean, which is exactly what variant A is trying to avoid. Toast briefly with cumin and coriander at the masala stage; scale the quantity to the variant.

CARDAMOM IS CRUCIAL IN BOTH VARIANTS — PREVIOUSLY UNDERVALUED: cardamom (green pods bruised, or a small pinch of ground) is more important in this recipe than I've historically given it credit for. It's the spice that ties both variants back to their indian-saag identity — in variant A it anchors the dish against the mediterranean drift the ajwain+feta axis threatens; in variant B it balances the fried-paneer richness with a warm floral background. Use it generously. This is a spice I've been dosing too lightly for years.

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER AND RED CHILI FLAKES ARE BOTH NECESSARY — SAME RULE AS THE MINT CILANTRO SKILLET (SEE MASTER-MINT-CILANTRO-SKILLET): the indian chile stack for both variants is generic chili powder + kashmiri chili powder + red chili flakes. Each does a different job:
- KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: brings bright red color and gentle warming heat without aggression. Non-optional.
- RED CHILI FLAKES: the whole-form chile presence. Bloom in the oil at the masala stage.
- GENERIC CHILI POWDER: covers the broad heat + earthy character.
Same combination that master-mint-cilantro-skillet uses; the palak-paneer-adjacent indian family wants the full chile stack.

ONIONS ARE CLASHING — ONE OF THE FEW INDIAN DISHES WHERE ONIONS ARE INAPPROPRIATE: most indian dishes lean heavily on a sautéed onion base — chana masala, rajma, tikka masala, coconut mung bean curry, chana madra, effectively the chole/chana masala/mediterranean dal/rajma family all require onion as structural aromatic. Master indian saag curry is one of the rare exceptions. Onions read heavier than what either variant wants — the pestled spinach + cheese + structural-additions composition is already dense enough, and onions add a redundant sweetness plus a caramelized bass note. Strong stance — the recipe genuinely reads better without onions. Yellow, red, and white onions are all clashing here. Scallions and shallots stay out too by default.

GREAT IN TORTILLAS / THIN WRAPS / QUESADILLA STRUCTURES: both variants work exceptionally well as a wrap or quesadilla filling. The cheese-plus-vegetables (variant A) or paneer-plus-spinach (variant B) composition already reads gratin-adjacent, so folding into a flour tortilla or thin flatbread and pan-crisping the outside gives a portable, structured version. The lime and chili powder read especially well in this format. Variant A pairs particularly well with quesadilla-style folding thanks to the melted soft cheese. If making tortillas from scratch, see the flour tortillas recipe.

TECHNIQUE NOTES:
- Mortar + pestle the spinach (or finely dice). Never blend to babyfood.
- Chili powder + kashmiri + flakes are all necessary — don't skip.
- No sunflower or pumpkin seeds — they pull this toward a pesto-adjacent dish, which isn't what we want.
